guest_info.name: Kelsi Sheren
guest_info.title: Canadian Forces veteran, artillery gunner, Founder of Brass in Unity

summary: Kelsi Sheren, a Canadian Forces veteran, discusses her experiences as an artillery gunner in Afghanistan, her struggles with PTSD, and her work with Brass in Unity to support veterans. The conversation also explores historical perspectives on war, the psychological impact of trauma, and potential solutions for supporting veterans and preventing future conflicts.

main_entities.name: Brass in Unity
main_entities.context: Jewelry company founded by Kelsi Sheren that supports veteran rehabilitation.

main_entities.name: M777 Howitzer
main_entities.context: Artillery gun used by Kelsi Sheren in Afghanistan; details of its operation and transportation are discussed.

main_entities.name: Afghanistan
main_entities.context: The country where Kelsi Sheren served and experienced combat, leading to PTSD.

main_entities.name: PTSD
main_entities.context: Post-traumatic stress disorder experienced by Kelsi Sheren and other veterans; discussed in terms of symptoms, triggers, and treatment.

main_entities.name: World War II
main_entities.context: Historical conflict discussed in relation to civilian casualties, the rise of dictators, and lessons for the present.

main_entities.name: Taliban
main_entities.context: The insurgent group in Afghanistan; their tactics, motivations, and impact on the civilian population are discussed.

main_entities.name: Heroic Hearts
main_entities.context: Charity that provides psychedelic treatments for veterans with PTSD; Kelsi Sheren participated in an ayahuasca ceremony with them.

main_entities.name: Ayahuasca
main_entities.context: Psychedelic brew used in a ceremony to help Kelsi Sheren process trauma and overcome PTSD.

main_entities.name: Kandahar
main_entities.context: Major base in Afghanistan where Kelsi Sheren was stationed; described as a relatively safe space with amenities.

main_entities.name: FOB Ramrod
main_entities.context: Forward Observation Base where Kelsi Sheren was stationed; a small, American-run base where she experienced combat.

main_entities.name: Operation Maple Leaf
main_entities.context: Training operation in Wainwright, Alberta, simulating combat scenarios in Afghanistan.

main_entities.name: Excalibur round
main_entities.context: GPS-guided, rocket-propelled artillery round used for VIP targets; costs half a million dollars per round.

key_details.fact: Kelsi Sheren was 18 years old when she served in Afghanistan.
Kelsi Sheren was officially told she was deploying on graduation day.
The barrel of an M777 Howitzer is worth a million dollars.
The Taliban used IEDs and vehicle-borne IEDs as standard ways to attack.
The people that were at the FOB before Kelsi Sheren left a noose hanging from the tent.
Kelsi Sheren was medically honorably discharged in 2011.
Kelsi Sheren was told by Trudeau that she was asking for too much when she lost her leg.
The Canadian government gave $10.4 million to an Afghan terrorist that was in the Canadian prison system.
The USA paid over $6 trillion for the wars in the Middle East since 9/11.

other_persons_mentioned.context: Compared to Hitler as a different flavor of evil during World War II
